News.title: Shocking victim bodycam footage captures moment Scots shoplifter struggles with Asda security guard before killing him
News.content:
THIS is the shocking moment a Scots shoplifter struggled with an Asda security guard before killing him as she tried to steal booze.

Horror bodycam footage has been released of thug Natasha Smith hurling abuse and pushing guard Alan Harris-Richards, who tried to stop her stealing alcohol from the supermarket.





Smith, 38, shouted “You’re not allowed to touch me” and “Get your f***ing hands off me”.

She screamed “Who do you think you are, f***ing Hulk?” while wrestling for the bag of booze.

The frenzied shoplifter’s aggressive and threatening behaviour resulted in the security guard, 61, suffering a fatal heart attack.

The tragedy occurred at the Asda supermarket at Westbury Retail Park in Arbroath on June 30, 2024.

Natasha Smith has today been caged for five years and four months at the High Court in Edinburgh.

During the fatal incident, the thief levelled a false claim against the victim of assaulting her as she tried to leave the shop with the bottles she was stealing.

A judge told Smith: “On June 30 in 2024 you set in train a series of events that ultimately had devastating consequences for Alun Harris-Richards, his family and all who knew and loved him.”



Lady Haldane said at the High Court in Edinburgh: “Mr Harris-Richards lost his life that day when he was simply doing his job as a security guard in a supermarket.”

The judge pointed out that Smith also assaulted another member of staff who was also simply doing her duty in trying to stop Smith stealing from the store.

Lady Haldane said she had received moving victim impact statements from members of the deceased’s family and added: “It is clear Mr Harris-Richards was a much loved member of the family who was deeply involved with his children and grandchildren.”

The judge said she recognised nothing she said or did would in any way lessen the sense of loss.

Lady Haldane said a background report prepared on Smith showed that she had suffered trauma in her own life and significant adverse experiences in childhood.



She told Smith that nothing could take away from the fact that Mr Harris-Richards would not have lost his life in the way he did but for her actions.

Lady Haldane told her that she would have faced a six year jail term for killing her victim, but that would be reduced following her guilty plea.

Smith, formerly of North Grimsby, Arbroath, earlier admitted killing Mr Harris-Richards on June 30 in 2024 at his workplace at the supermarket.



The tragic worker, who had undiagnosed heart disease, fell to the ground during the ordeal, hit his head and died instantly.

The court heard that Smith shouted and swore, made the false allegation against the victim, claimed that he would be prosecuted and repeatedly tried to pull a bag from him.

After the security guard fell to the floor, Smith continued to struggle with two women employees at the store and grabbed one of them, Chloe Robinson, and put her hands around her neck and told her: “I will end you.”

They managed to grab the bag from Smith who walked back towards the victim before she was pushed into the foyer by the women workers.

This resulted in an alarm being triggered, with staff suspecting she had further stolen goods in a handbag and after a further struggle that bag fell to the floor.



The thief continued to shout and threaten Ms Robinson, claiming: “I will end you” and punched her on the face and pulled her hair before leaving and being detained by police.

The charge said that as a result Mr Harris-Richards, who had an undiagnosed medical condition, “suffered such physical and emotional stress that he then and there sustained a heart attack, fell to the ground, struck his head to his injury and died and you did kill him”.

Smith was originally charged with attacking the victim by repeatedly pushing him on the body but the Crown amended the charge before she made her guilty plea to culpable homicide, by removing the assault allegation.

Benefit claimant Smith has amassed a record of criminal offending for repeated shoplifting, theft, drugs and assault in both Scotland and England but has never previously served a prison sentence, the court heard.

Moira Orr, who leads on homicide for the Crown Office and Procurator Fiscal Service (COPFS), said: “This was an unprovoked attack on a man who was simply doing his job.



“Natasha Smith’s actions resulted in tragic consequences and robbed a much-loved family man of his life.

“She will now serve a prison sentence for her crime.

“This conviction serves as a warning to others that we take crimes of this nature extremely seriously and will use all the tools available to us to seek justice.

“Our thoughts lie with Mr Harris-Richards’ family and friends. We are sorry for their loss.”

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Detective Chief Inspector Craig Kelly said: “This was a shocking and tragic incident which resulted in the death of a man at his workplace.



“Aggressive behaviour of this nature is completely unacceptable and can have devastating consequences, as this case has shown.

News.title: Shocking moment naked tourist on ‘drug-fuelled crime spree’ is tasered by police on holiday beach
News.content:
THIS is the moment a naked tourist was tasered and arrested on a notorious holiday island after terrorising other travellers in a nude beach rampage.

The German man sparked panic on Thailand‘s “Death Island” after stealing a woman’s phone, a motorbike and a kayak – all without clothes on.





Koh Tao police allege the man began behaving erratically on Wednesday morning, starting his drug-fuelled spree by approaching a woman outside a 7-Eleven.

Body-warn camera footage shows the officers catching up with him around 6pm on Chalok Beach on the southern part of the island.

Cops can be seen on speedboats trying to stop the naked suspect as he makes a break for the shoreline.



Officers were forced to fire a taser at him when he refused to listen.

One of the cops on the scene said the full details were still being investigated.

“Several victims may need to come forward and file complaints, as he was involved in multiple incidents,” the officer from the tactical squad of Provincial Police Region 8 said.

“It started when he approached a woman outside a 7-Eleven and asked for her Whatsapp.



“When she refused, he pretended to ask to check a map, then suddenly grabbed her phone and sped off on a motorbike.”

Police say he later stole a motorcycle and then a kayak.

“As a result, the team from Provincial Police Region 8 had to pursue him by speedboat,” the officer said.

“[He] tried to swim away in the sea around the island before coming ashore on Chalok Beach.

“We used a Taser X7 to safely control the situation. Non-lethal equipment is extremely important in places like this.”



The man was detained by Koh Tao rescuers and later taken to the hospital.

He remains in police custody as the investigation continues.

The officer said police deal with similar situations to this “almost every night”.

Hotel receptionist Kotchakorn Baipimai said the naked man had been reported for following two women trying to check into a hotel on the island.

“At first, I thought he came with them, but something felt wrong,” Baipimai said.



“The two women were not speaking and looked very frightened, as if they were about to cry.”

She said the man had attempted to get close to the women, so told him to leave.

“I do not know whether he was drunk or under the influence of something,” Baipimai added.

“He eventually went outside and rode off on a motorbike.”

She added that the man had been wearing underwear at the time of the ordeal.



“When we asked the guests what had happened, they said they had noticed him already parked on a motorbike behind the shuttle vehicle when they arrived,” she said.

“As soon as they got out and walked in to check in, he started following them.

“The guests said it was their first time visiting Koh Tao when this happened.”

Koh Tao is one of the most popular scuba diving locations in the world.

It was dubbed “Death Island” due to the high number of tourists who have died in mysterious circumstances.



It is estimated that dozens of people have died, however official numbers are suspected to be higher, covered up to protect the island’s lucrative tourism industry.

The highest profile case was the death of Hannah Witheridge and David Miller in 2014.

The pair were bludgeoned to death on Sairee Beach.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Two Burmese labourers were allegedly framed for the murders in order to protect the real killer – believed to be the son of a prominent local family.

The case was wrapped up quickly amidst unwanted international scrutiny.

News.title: ‘Experienced’ climber, 52, who plunged 130ft to his death at UK beauty spot is pictured as family pay tribute
News.content:
AN experienced climber plunged 130ft from cliffs at a coastal beauty spot.

Postie Justin Lowman was tackling St Govan’s Head on the Pembrokeshire coast with two mates at 4pm on February 12 when he died.





The 52-year-old – who was a “very accomplished climber” – died despite a massive rescue attempt.

The two others were taken to hospital after emergency services rushed to the coast.

Justin’s family paid tribute to the “much loved, son, brother and uncle” today.

A family spokesperson said: “Justin grew up in Pembrokeshire from the age of five and had a love of the outdoors from an early age, enjoying climbing, cycling and running.



“He was a very experienced and accomplished climber and well known in the local climbing community.

“He went to Tasker Milward school followed by Liverpool University. He worked at Cardiff Council, Pembrokeshire College and latterly for the Royal Mail.

St Govan’s Head in west Wales is famed for its towering limestone sea cliffs, which rise 131 ft above the sea, and dramatic views.

It is located within the Pembrokeshire Coast National Park, a site renowned for its natural beauty and popular with hikers and climbers.



There are as many as 300 recognised climbing routes, with some graded beyond “Hard Very Severe”, according to the British Mountaineering Council.

In some areas there are unstable rocks and the cliffs are closed to climbing in spring and summer months due to nesting sea birds.

St Govan’s is famous for a hidden chapel built into the cliffs near Bosherston, in South Pembrokeshire.

The tiny chapel dates back to the 13th Century and was built to honour the hermit St Govan.

Dyfed-Powys Police said officers were contacted by the ambulance service over concerns for the welfare of three people on the coast.



Justin, from Steynton, was pronounced dead at the scene.

The force said the death was not being treated as suspicious and next of kin have been informed.

News.title: Perv who lured girl, 15, to flat before she was raped & murdered will be FREED from jail – as teen’s mum slams decision
News.content:
MUM'S FURY Perv who lured girl, 15, to flat before she was raped & murdered will be FREED from jail – as teen’s mum slams decision

A PERV who lured a 15-year-old girl to a flat where she was raped and murdered will be freed from jail in just weeks.

Kayleigh Haywood and Luke Harlow, 27, exchanged 2,643 text messages in just 13 days as he groomed the lovestruck teen.





The fiend held her captive in his flat in Ibstock, Leicester, before telling his neighbour Stephen Beadman he had a “bird” over.

Beadman raped and battered the teenager to death with a brick then dumped her body in a field.

Harlow is now set to be released from prison on licence at the start of May in a move which has infuriated Kayleigh’s mum Stephanie.



She said: “So news I have had today, this scumbag is being released early under the new laws of the Ministry of Justice.

“He is due to be released the week commencing May 4 2026 but has already been recalled once for reoffending for being the dirty b*****d that he will always be so this country is f***ed up.

“Let’s just allow dirty predators to walk our streets not think of the safety of our kids or young adults…the fact is he comes out and lives a life, something my daughter can’t.

“How is this fair? How is this right? No justice at all, makes me physically sick.”



Harlow was jailed for 12 years in 2016 for grooming, sexual activity with a child and false imprisonment. He confessed to grooming two other girls.

He was later released but recalled back to prison in July 2023 after he failed to stick to the agreed terms of his licence.

The gruesome killing took place in 2015 after Harlow – then aged 27 – began contacting Kayleigh on Facebook.

On November 13 that year, she told her parents she was staying with a friend but instead spent the night at Harlow’s home believing she was on a date.

She rang her parents the next day but later a text message was sent from her phone to a friend reading: “Help”.



Kayleigh was subjected to a terrifying six-hour ordeal after Beadman arrived the next morning.

The evil pair plied Kayleigh with booze and abused her as the teenager spent her final hours trapped in the house of horrors.

Brave Kayleigh attempted to flee the house but was chased and caught by Beadman.

He marched her half-naked through freezing woods where he raped her before battering her to death.

Her body was discovered five days later in a field and had to be identified using dental records.



Beadman was arrested after a witness told police they saw Kayleigh running from the house – followed closely by the killer.

He later admitted killing and raping Kayleigh but he and Harlow forced her parents to endure a harrowing trial after they denied holding her captive.

The depraved duo were both convicted at Nottingham Crown Court in June 2016.

Beadman was caged for life with a minimum of 35 years for false imprisonment, rape and murder.

He died in prison in April 2021 after taking his own life.



Harlow will remain on licence until the end of his sentence and on the sex offenders’ register indefinitely.

Under the Victim Contact Scheme, those close to Kayleigh will have the right to make representations about licence conditions, including non-contact and exclusion zones.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

A Ministry of Justice spokesperson said: “Our thoughts remain with the family and friends of Kayleigh Haywood.

“Offenders released on licence must obey strict rules and can be recalled to prison if they break the rules”

News.title: Terrifying moment Iranian suicide drone nosedives into international airport as ANOTHER country is dragged into war
News.content:
IRAN has fired two drones into oil-rich ex-Soviet state Azerbaijan – dragging yet another country into its Middle East conflict.

Shocking footage shows the moment Nakhchivan International Airport – located just six miles from the Iranian border – was blasted by one of the deadly devices.





A second drone narrowly missed a school in the city, triggering an explosive warning from Baku that it “reserves the right to take retaliatory measures”.

Nakhchivan is the capital of a strategically sensitive Azerbaijani exclave, cut off from the rest of the country and wedged between Iran, Armenia, and Turkey.

Azerbaijan has deep security ties with Israel, including major arms deals and intelligence cooperation – links Tehran brands an Israeli “listening post” on its northern frontier.



Baku’s foreign ministry said: “Around noon, drone attacks were carried out on the Nakhchivan Autonomous Republic of Azerbaijan from the territory of the Islamic Republic of Iran.

“One drone fell on the airport terminal building, and another one landed near a school building in the village of Shekarabad.

It comes as…

“We strongly condemn these drone attacks launched from the territory of the Islamic Republic of Iran, which damaged the airport building and injured two civilians.



“This attack on Azerbaijani territory is contrary to the norms and principles of international law and contributes to escalating tensions in the region.

“We demand that the Islamic Republic of Iran promptly clarify the above-mentioned issue, provide an explanation, and take the necessary urgent measures to prevent the recurrence of such incidents.”

Azerbaijan said it “reserves the right to take retaliatory measures.”

Iran’s ambassador to the country was hauled in to answer questions from its enraged leaders.

Initial reports suggested the strike was carried out by Shahed drones, which have wreaked havoc on countries around the Gulf.



Volodymyr Zelensky offered to assist the US, European and Middle Eastern countries in combating these drones, which Russia has routinely used to bombard Ukraine.

Azerbaijan becomes the 12th country to be hit by Iran – and the tenth Muslim nation.

In an ironic twist, Azerbaijan’s president Ilham Aliyev yesterday visited the Iranian embassy in Baku to pay tribute to slain supreme leader Ali Khamenei.



The late tyrant was killed in a coordinated US-Israeli strike on his palace in Tehran on Saturday.

A full-blown war has broken out since, with Iran launching a wave of retaliatory strikes against Gulf neighbours who host US airbases.

Israel and Lebanon have also traded blows as Tehran’s chief proxy Hezbollah waded into the conflict.

Iran has also closed the vital Strait of Hormuz – through which 20% of the world’s oil passes – threatening the global economy and prompting terrified motorists to panic-buy petrol.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

This morning, a US tanker was engulfed in flames after an Iranian strike.



Tehran launched its bitter revenge after a US submarine sank an Iranian warship off the coast of Sri Lanka yesterday, killing 87.

News.title: Illegal migrant families to be paid up to £40k to leave UK within a week as Shabana Mahmood reveals migration overhaul
News.content:
ILLEGAL migrant families will be offered a whopping £40,000 to get out of Britain voluntarily.

Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood announced today that under a pilot scheme, 150 arrivals will be able to claim £10,000 per person – including kids – to leave within a week.





Outlining her immigration overhaul in Westminster, Ms Mahmood said: “Failed asylum seekers will now be offered an increased incentive payment of £10,000 per person, and up to a maximum of £40,000 per family.

“To put that in important context, today, a family of three asylum hotel accommodation costs up to £158,000 per year.

“Should these incentives prove effective, they will represent a significant saving to the taxpayer.

“Where a voluntary removal is refused, we will escalate to an enforced removal for those who can be returned to their safe home country.”

Ms Mahmood today threw down the gauntlet to squeamish Labour MPs as she unleashed her hardline migration crackdown.

She warned lefties in her party that “at the same time as showing generosity, we must wrest control over migration back from the smugglers and restore order and control at our border”.

It came as 100 Labour MPs signed a letter to the PM slamming proposals to crackdown on both lawful and illegal migration.

Referencing demands for Sir Keir to push his party leftward, the Home Secretary admitted that “we meet at a difficult time for my party”.



But she slapped down pleas to abandon her migration reforms.

Ms Mahmood said: “Of course, we should be more Labour. The real question is, what does more Labour mean, because, in my view, more Labour doesn’t mean more Green, just like more Labour doesn’t mean more Reform.

“More Labour means reconnecting with who we are, who we represent, and what we believe.”

The Home Secretary insisted her sweeping changes demonstrate both “compassion and control” and sits between the “extremes” of the Greens and Reform.

She added: “They are no invitation to the fairy tale of open borders as Zack Polanski’s revolutionary green party demands, and neither are they the nightmare of Nigel Farage pulling up the drawbridge and shutting out the world, narrowing our proud patriotism into a crude ethno nationalism.



“Between those two extremes there is ample space for a migration system that is fair, and where it needs to be firm.”

She launched a pilot scheme offering failed asylum-seeker families up to £40,000 each to leave Britain within seven days.

Those who refuse the offer risked being detained and forcibly removed instead.

Ministers said the payouts could save taxpayers millions, as some families cost up to £158,000 a year in hotels and support.

Under the new rules, migrants will also have to speak English to A-level standard before they can settle permanently in Britain from next year.



Refugees granted protection no longer receive automatic long-term status, but are instead placed on temporary 30-month protection visas subject to review.

Asylum seekers who brake the law, work illegally or can support themselves lose taxpayer-funded accommodation and cash support.

Officials said the measures will help bring down the eye-watering £4 billion asylum support bill.

The Home Secretary declared the changes were needed to restore “order and control” to Britain’s borders.

She said the reforms struck a balance between “Farage’s nightmare pulling up the drawbridge and shutting out the world” and “Polanski’s fairy tale of open borders”.



In the speech, she also insisted restoring control of migration was “an embodiment” of Labour values.

But the package is already sparking anger among left-wing Labour MPs who warned the government risked going too far.

Some of the most controversial reforms to the settlement system are expected to appear in primary legislation in the King’s Speech in May under her proposed “earned settlement model”.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

This will see the normal route to settlement double from five years to ten for most migrants, with faster tracks for those who made a strong contribution to Britain’s economy and society.

Under the plans, skilled frontline public service workers such as doctors and nurses will still qualify after five years.

News.title: Elite Ukrainian drone hunters to be deployed to help Gulf fend off Iran drone onslaught as Brits frantically try to flee
News.content:
UKRAINE’S drone experts will help Gulf states defend against the barrage of attacks from Iran.

President Volodymyr Zelensky offered his help to partners in the Middle East, even as Mad Vlad continues to pummel Ukraine with drone attacks.





Zelensky will send his elite drone hunters to Gulf nations to share their experience of shooting down enemy aircraft.

The Ukrainian president said: “Our military possesses the necessary capabilities.

“Ukrainian experts will work on site, and the teams are already discussing this.”



The deadly drones being launched at US bases and other targets across the Middle East are the same as those that Putin has relentlessly aimed at Ukrainians.

No country in the world has better experience at striking down these drones.

Zelensky revealed: “And we are ready to help protect lives, protect civilians, and support real efforts to stabilise the situation and restore, in particular, safe shipping in the region.

“Ukraine is conducting consultations with partners in Europe and the United States, as well as with Iran‘s neighbouring countries.”



He has spoken to the leaders of the UAE, Bahrain, Qatar, and Jordan – all of which are facing daily onslaughts of drones as Iran wreaks its revenge for US-Israeli operation Epic Fury.

Follow The Sun’s live blog for the latest updates on Iran

The Ukrainian president added: “There will be further talks with Kuwait and other countries in the region.

“All of them face serious challenges and speak openly about them.

“Iranian attack drones – the same Shahed drones that have been attacking our cities and villages, our Ukrainian infrastructure for years during this war.”



It comes as Ukraine asked the US about potentially pushing back or changing the location of the next round of peace talks with Russia.

The next talks mediated by the US were planned for 5 March – 9 March, but chaos in the Middle East could disrupt them.



Zelensky said: “The Iranian regime, which is trying to survive at all costs, poses obvious threats to all states in the region, as well as to global stability.

It comes as…

“No state neighbouring Iran can feel safe. The Iranian regime has not yet demonstrated any real intention of engaging in honest diplomacy or making fundamental changes.”

He ordered his military and intelligence commanders “to present options for support to relevant states and assistance in such a way that it does not weaken our own defence here in Ukraine”.

He called for backing from leading Western states to assist this effort.



RECOMMENDED STORIES

It comes as the first repatriation flight slated to save British tourists stranded in the Middle East was grounded this morning.

The flight was supposed to depart Muscat, Oman, at 11pm local time (7pm UK time) on Wednesday – but instead, after enduring agonising waits, Brits were sent back to their hotels.



﻿

News.title: Teenager and man in his 20s arrested over viral ‘school wars’ craze as cops warn parents over violent social media trend
News.content:
SCHOOL WARS Teenager and man in his 20s arrested over viral ‘school wars’ craze as cops warn parents over violent social media trend

A TEENAGE boy and a man in his 20s have been arrested in connection with the viral “school wars” trend which urged children to be violent.

The posts pitted pupils from rival schools against each other and told students as young as 11 to get weapons and be ready for “war”.





The Metropolitan Police arrested the boy, 15, and the man on Sunday on suspicion of encouraging or assisting in a crime, they have both been released on bail.

Posts rapidly circulated last month across several platforms dividing schools into team red and team blue, inspired by the notorious Bloods and Crips gangs.

The images showed masked figures or household items that could be used as weapons and sparked concerns for parents across London.

Boroughs in the capital were pitted against each other as Year 7 to Year 11 schools were divided into the two camps.



Viral TikTok posts from anonymous accounts saw pupils being urged to bring compasses and combs ready to fight each other.

Parents were advised to collect children from the school gates and many expressed fears on Facebook.

A spokesperson for the force said: “On Sunday, 1 March at approximately 18:45hrs, Met officers arrested two individuals in Central East London in connection with a recent trend of social media posts.”

They added: “A strong police presence remains around schools, particularly at the end of the school day.



“Local officers continue to work alongside local authority partners, school leaders and parents, whose joint robust response has been vital in protecting young people across the last week.”

News.title: Warning to all drivers as councils crackdown on pavement parking with new rules
News.content:
Warning to all drivers as councils crackdown on pavement parking with new rules

Local authorities in England will be given new powers to tackle pavement parking, making it simpler for councils to impose restrictions across wider areas than before

View 2 Images The new rules will apply to cars parked on pavements (Image: Raylipscombe via Getty Images)

Local authorities are set to receive fresh powers to address the issue of pavement parking, making it easier for them to enforce restrictions.

‌



The Department for Transport declared in January that "new and improved legal powers" will allow councils to limit pavement parking over much broader areas than previously possible.

‌



Under the old regulations, authorities were required to request pavement parking restrictions, often on a street-by-street basis, which slowed down the process. The modification is designed to ensure pavements remain accessible for vulnerable individuals such as wheelchair users, parents with pushchairs, and those with visual impairments.

‌



Nonetheless, local authorities will retain the discretion to determine where enforcement is most needed, enabling them to "retain flexibility where they deem pavement parking may still be acceptable".

NHS GP shares 1 crucial mistake hay fever sufferers make with antihistamines READ MORE :

View 2 Images Local authorities will be given more power (Image: PeterEtchells via Getty Images)

Pavement parking laws differ throughout the UK, with some regions enforcing stricter controls than others.

‌



In London, parking on pavements has been banned since 1974, with penalties reaching up to £130 for infringements. Beyond the capital, there's no sweeping nationwide prohibition on pavement parking – local authorities can only intervene when a vehicle violates other regulations, such as stopping on double yellow lines or where particular restrictions are implemented and clearly marked.

Mounting a pavement is generally prohibited under Section 72 of the Highways Act 1835, but once stationary, it doesn't automatically constitute an offence unless it creates an obstruction, which typically falls under police jurisdiction rather than council authority.

Scotland rolled out a countrywide ban in December 2023, although comprehensive enforcement won't kick in until April 2026, with fines beginning at £100 (dropping to £50 for prompt payment).

‌



In Wales, comparable restrictions exist under the Highways Act, but enforcement frequently depends on local authorities implementing traffic regulation orders (TROs) for particular locations.

Whilst the modifications aren't scheduled to take effect until later this year, there's been widespread backing for stricter rules governing pavement parking. However, Tom Hunt, the chair of the Local Government Association's inclusive growth committee and the head of Sheffield council, expressed to Yahoo News that local authorities will require adequate resources to enforce these regulations.

He stated: "These new powers – something the LGA has long called for – will help councils keep pavements clear."

‌



Pavement parking is one of the most complained about issues by residents – it can cause a lot of disruption and block access, particularly to those with mobility issues or parents who are pushing their children in buggies. They also cause cracked slabs and, therefore, increase maintenance costs and trip hazards.

"Whilst these new powers are positive, they must be accompanied by the requisite resources to implement and enforce effectively, at a time when council budgets are already stretched."

Chris Theobald, senior policy, public affairs and campaigns manager at the Guide Dogs charity, told Yahoo News that he welcomed the progress on pavement parking "after years of inaction", revealing that 95% of people with sight loss have been "forced to walk in the road", whilst a fifth have sustained injuries due to vehicles obstructing pavements.

‌



He said: "Being forced into the road even once is dangerous, but being regularly stopped from travelling safely has a real impact on people's ability to live and work independently."

Theobald noted that the government proposals would grant councils enhanced powers to determine how best to address the problem – whether through implementing an outright ban, similar to London, or pursuing more focused enforcement measures.

He added: "For people with sight loss, consistency is important, so we're keen to avoid a confusing and complex patchwork of rules. People with sight loss should be able to use the pavement safely wherever they live, work or travel. We believe these proposals need to be developed and powers given to councils swiftly, and shown to be effective.

‌



"The laws in place in Scotland and London have been proven to work, and their example should be followed if the alternatives are less effective."

RAC senior policy officer Rod Dennis told Yahoo News UK that their research discovered a majority of motorists backed tougher action against thoughtless parking which forces pedestrians off pavements and into traffic. He continued: "These proposals should clear the way for councils to prevent pavement parking where it causes problems, but permit drivers to partially park on pavements where doing so helps keep traffic flowing and doesn't inconvenience other people.

"However, it's vital councils inform drivers that they'll be starting to enforce unnecessary pavement parking, and are reasonable when doing so.

Article continues below

"As it will be for individual authorities to judge what constitutes an 'unnecessary obstruction of the pavement' and what doesn't, drivers must be treated fairly – or there's a risk councils start receiving a high volume of appeals for fines."

News.title: Devoted nun dies in Wales after being hit by car
News.content:
Tributes have poured in for a devoted nun who tragically died after a car slammed into her as she crossed the road.

Sister Genevieve, 87, was rushed to hospital after the collision but she tragically succumbed to her injuries and later died. The Sisters of St. Joseph of Annecy in Newport shared their "great sadness" after hearing the news of St Genevieve's death.

The nun, who was previously headteacher of St. Winifred's School in Lee in south London, died after she was struck on Stow Park Avenue, Newport, just before 4pm on Tuesday, February 24.

Gwent Police are investigating the crash and have not yet made any arrests. The force has appealed for witnesses or anyone with relevant information, including CCTV or dashcam footage, to contact them.

Image: Google Maps) Google Maps)

This is a breaking news story.

News.title: Petrol forecourts 'profiteering' as fuel prices jump nearly 9p a litre
News.content:
Petrol forecourts 'profiteering' as fuel prices jump nearly 9p a litre

Lib Dems urge watchdogs to warn fuel giants that they could be on the hook for hefty fines 'if they are found to be ripping off consumers'

View 3 Images Drivers have been urged not to rush to fill up amid some signs of queues at forecourts (Image: Men Media)

Forecourts have been warned against profiting from soaring oil costs amid claims that some have jacked up pump prices by nearly 9p a litre in recent days.

‌



Motorists have begun to notice the price of petrol and diesel begin to rise amid the fall-out from the Middle East conflict.

‌



The RAC says the average price of petrol increased by nearly 2.5p a litre, and diesel by more than 3p, between last Saturday and this Wednesday. But there are reports of prices on some forecourts rising by much more.

‌



Howard Cox, founder of the group FairFuelUK, warned against the risk of “opportunistic profiteering”. He said it had been contacted by more than 120 campaign supporters form the across the UK who have reported that pump prices have increased in the past 48 hours by an average of 6.7p for petrol and 8.8p for diesel.

“Most of these forecourts, many believe, are selling fuel at these higher prices even though they bought these stocks before any wholesale rises,” he said. “It seems opportunistic profiteering is rife once again.”

Daisy Cooper, Lib Dem Treasury spokesperson, said: “People have already been paying through the nose to fill up their car, as forecourts have jacked up their profit margins over a period of months. Last time oil prices went up, petrol stations raised their profits even more, so people are now worried that this could happen yet again, at an even greater scale.

‌



View 3 Images Supporters of the group FairFuelUk claim to have seen petrol rise by an average 6.7p and diesel by 8.8p a litre in recent days (Image: ANP/Shutterstock)

“Fuel giants should not be allowed to treat families already struggling with the cost of living like cash cows. The competition watchdog must step in and warn fuel giants that they could be on the hook for hefty fines if they are found to be ripping off consumers.”

Gordon Balmer, executive director of the Petrol Retailers Association, said: “The conflict in the Middle East has increased the wholesale cost of petrol and diesel, which will mean pump prices will have to go up. Rising fuel prices hurt the economy in the form of higher inflation, impacting already hard-pressed household budgets.”

‌



The PRA has written to Chancellor Rachel Reeves urging her to scrap a planned fuel duty rise later this year.

View 3 Images Lib Dem Treasury spokesperson Daisy Cooper warned fuel firms against using drivers as 'cash cows' (Image: Getty Images)

Oil prices rose further on Thursday, with Brent crude trading at more than $83 a barrel. The sharp rise - along with a big increase in wholesale gas costs - has also led to fears that households energy bills could jump later this year.

‌



Dan Coatsworth, head of markets at broker AJ Bell, said: “Brent Crude continued to move higher, nudging above $83 per barrel and stoking fears that energy bills will go through the roof. Oil is so important to the world’s economy and to see the price go up so quickly in just a week could leave investors feeling dazed and confused.

“The Middle East situation is unfolding at a rapid pace, and investors are finding it hard to make a firm call on whether there will be a sustained energy crisis or just a short, sharp shock.”

Crude oil markets remained on edge as they face ongoing risks to supply following the attacks in the Middle East, with concerns centred on trade flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

News.title: Should Brits pay for their flights back from Dubai? Take our poll and have your say
News.content:
Should Brits pay for their flights back from Dubai? Take our poll and have your say

Tens of thousands of Brits are stranded in the Middle East after Iran launched a series of missile and drone strikes against nations that are allied with the US

View 3 Images Flights have been cancelled across the Middle East (Image: Marcin Golba/NurPhoto/Shutterstock)

British holidaygoers are still stranded in Dubai with tens of thousands needing to be evacuated from the Middle East amid Iran's air strikes - do you think they should pay for their flights back to the UK?

‌



The Middle East was thrown into chaos on Saturday when Donald Trump and Benjamin Netanyahu ordered strikes on the Iranian capital, Tehran. They had a devastating impact on the tyrannical regime, with Ayatollah Ali Khamenei being killed in strikes on his compound.

‌



Iran responded by unleashing volleys of missile and drone attacks on nations aligned or stationing US bases on its territory. The impact has led to at least 138,000 Brits being stranded across the Middle East with many desperately seeking a way to return home.

‌



View 3 Images Some Brits have managed to arrive home (Image: PA)

Sirens blare at UK military base in Cyprus amid possible missile security alert READ MORE :

Brits now face weighing up whether, or even how, they will be able to get to Oman where flights are currently scheduled to return to the UK. Many stranded Brits have been forced to fork out thousands of pounds to remain in their hotel rooms and with unclear guidance being given about how they can leave.

‌



The Mirror is now asking in a poll whether those people who need to return home should pay for their flights out of Dubai. The UK Government has, so far, not indicated when any flights will be leaving the United Arab Emirates.

View 3 Images Iran launched strikes against Dubai (Image: @TheAlexJacob/X)

It said it was "extremely difficult" to get Brits back home due to airspace closures and restrictions. The government added it was "working around the clock" with airlines, travel companies and regional governments to find safe routes out for Brits.

Article continues below

Prime Minister Keir Starmer said in the House of Commons on Wednesday: "One thousand British nationals arrived back in the UK on commercial flights from the UAE. A further eight flights are due to leave the UAE to the UK. Today, I can confirm, Mr. Speaker, that the first charter flight is expected to leave Oman later today, and two more will come in the coming days."

UK Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper said: "Hundreds of thousands of Brits are affected by the Middle East conflict - including holidaymakers who can’t get home whilst airspace is restricted or closed. The Government is working 24/7 with airlines and regional governments to pursue extra flights and safe routes."

News.title: Four-bedroom house in picturesque village where homes sell for £750,000 on sale for £19,000
News.content:
Four-bedroom house in picturesque village where homes sell for £750,000 on sale for £19,000

It could be a chance to grab a bargain

View 4 Images Bidding starts at £19,000 (Image: Jam Press/Paul Fosh Auctions)

A four-bedroom house in a stunning village where homes fetch more than £700,000 could be yours for as little as £19,000. The detached dwelling is billed as an ideal family residence or lucrative holiday rental investment.

‌



It sits amidst undulating hills, waterways and dales. The property stands in Aysgarth, within the Yorkshire Dales National Park.

‌



Homes in the area routinely command prices reaching £750,000, according to Luxury Property News.

‌



The listing states: "Located in Aysgarth, a charming village in North Yorkshire, this four-bedroom property is in an ideal location. A beautiful family home or a bolt hole or holiday let as an investment opportunity - it's one not to be missed.

"It has a large front garden with double garages and three separate access points in to the property. It also has a side courtyard exiting from the lounge.

View 4 Images The kitchen (Image: Jam Press/Paul Fosh Auctions)

‌



"Upstairs, this property has three large double bedrooms, two with en-suites, one Jack and Jill bathroom and a single bedroom with a built in storage cupboard, plus a family bathroom. There is also an attic for storage etcetera.

"Downstairs, you have a large living or dining room with a fireplace, as well as a w/c, under stairs storage cupboard, large kitchen area with stable door and a large utility area leading to the double garage. Houses in this area typically sell for around £625,000-£760,000 on the open market."

The listing noted the property has the potential to bring in £1,300-£1,500 monthly. As a holiday rental venture, it could yield £1,800-£2,100 weekly when listed on platforms like Airbnb or Booking.com, particularly for those happy to welcome pets and similar guests.

‌



View 4 Images The bathroom (Image: Jam Press/Paul Fosh Auctions)

The description continued: "Aysgarth and its surroundings are set in the stunning Yorkshire Dales National Park, one of the UK's most picturesque and sought-after countryside locations. Surrounded by rolling hills, rivers and valleys, typical of the iconic Yorkshire Dales landscape.

"The Dales are famous for picturesque villages, lush walking trails, and panoramic views - ideal for buyers seeking countryside living or holiday accommodation potential. Close to the Peak District National Park, one of the UK's most visited National Parks, attracting around 13 million visitors each year.

‌



View 4 Images A spacious lounge (Image: Jam Press/Paul Fosh Auctions)

"A huge draw for walkers, climbers, cyclists, families and international tourists with dramatic gritstone edges, limestone dales and rolling hills.

"Known for outdoor activities like hiking iconic trails, cycling scenic routes, nature-watching and visiting historic houses and market towns. Many buyers and holidaymakers explore both areas during visits."

Article continues below

Paul Fosh Auctions will handle the sale between Tuesday, March 10, and Thursday, March 12.

News.title: Prince William's secret outing with two major royals for poignant reason revealed
News.content:
Prince William's secret outing with two major royals for poignant reason revealed

Prince William made an under-the-radar trip to Wales yesterday with his aunts Princess Anne and Sophie, the Duchess of Edinburgh, for a special memorial service

View 5 Images Prince William (Image: Getty Images)

Prince William made an under-the-radar appearance with two other senior royals to remember a close family friend. The Prince of Wales travelled to Brecon in Wales yesterday, where he attended a memorial service for Dame Shan Legge-Bourke. She had a sweet connection to both William and his brother, Prince Harry, as her daughter Tiggy was their childhood nanny.

‌



Tiggy grew extremely close to the brothers, with the pair regular visitors to Dame Shan's Glanusk estate on the Welsh border. At her memorial yesterday at Brecon Cathedral, William was joined by his aunts Princess Anne and Sophie, the Duchess of Edinburgh. Dame Shan was a former lady-in-waiting for Anne.

‌



Princess Kate surprises with historic first as she and William issue new video READ MORE :

5 View 5 Images Prince William and Princess Anne were spotted heading to the memorial service (Image: X) 5 View 5 Images William chatted to pupils from Christ College Brecon (Image: X)

‌



According to the Court Circular, William attended the memorial on behalf of his father, the King, while Sophie was there in place of her husband, Prince Edward, the Duke of Edinburgh. On their way to the service, both William and Anne passed through Christ College Brecon, where a crowd of pupils came out to greet them. In mobile phone footage, William could be seen asking the children, "No school work?", before joking, "Surely, it's exam time, it must be".

Dame Shan died in December aged 82. She inherited the huge swathe of land from her father, William Bailey, the 3rd Lord Glanusk and was a pivotal figure in public life in Wales.

Over the years, she had been the Lord Lieutenant of Powys, the High Sheriff of Powys, President of Save the Children in Wales, President of the Royal Welsh Agricultural Society and the National Federation of Young Farmers Clubs.

‌



Announcing her death at the end of last year, her devastated family said: "We are very sad to announce the death of the Honourable Dame Shân Legge-Bourke DCVO CstJ, who died peacefully at home surrounded by all her children.

5 View 5 Images Prince Harry with Dame Shan Legge-Bourke (Image: WireImage) 5 View 5 Images Dame Shan with the late Queen in 2012 (Image: Getty Images)

‌



"We are devastated to lose our mother. She was an inspiration to us all, and to many people she met over her years of service to Wales, and the countless organisations and institutions she supported. A country girl at heart, she has left an indelible legacy at Glanusk, the wider countryside and beyond."

In 2006, Dame Shan appeared in a four-part documentary on BBC Wales called The Lady of Glanusk, where she was seen climbing on the roof of her home to clean the guttering and plucking pheasants with her seven grandchildren.

Her other daughter, Zara, remarked at the time: "She can drive a tractor better than any man I've ever seen and can reverse it through a narrow gap with a trailer on the back of it." Dame Shan had long been a friend of the royals, coming to know William and Harry well through her daughter, Tiggy, who once referred to the young princes as 'my babies'.

‌



Last year, Tiggy's stepson, Edward Pettifer, was killed in a terror attack in New Orleans. He was killed in the early hours of New Year's Day when a rented SUV rammed into a crowd on the city's famous Bourbon Street.

The 31-year-old, from Chelsea in west London, was the eldest son of Charles Pettifer, 59, a former Coldstream Guards officer who is now married to Tiggy, and Camilla Wyatt, 58, the daughter of a racehorse breeder.

Article continues below

At the time, William paid tribute and said: "Catherine and I have been shocked and saddened by the tragic death of Ed Pettifer. Our thoughts and prayers remain with the Pettifer family and all those innocent people who have been tragically impacted by this horrific attack.”

News.title: 'I organised iconic Andrew moment that led to Beatrice getting two-word nickname'
News.content:
'I organised iconic Andrew moment that led to Beatrice getting two-word nickname'

Sam McAlister, the Newsnight producer behind the infamous Pricne Andrew interview that went a long way towards destroying his reputation, credits Princess Beatrice with making it happen

View 3 Images Beatrice and her father remain close

Prince Andrew, as he was then called, attempted to clear his name in a notorious one-on-one Newsnight interview with presenter Emily Maitlis. That episode marked the beginning of the end for the House of York.

‌



Sam McAlister, the producer who negotiated the terms of that interview in 2019, has acknowledged its lasting influence in a new video for Palace Confidential: She said: “Who could have believed that, all these years later, we still have these many repercussions that come directly and indirectly from that 2019 BBC Newsnight interview?”

‌



The interview was over a year in the making, and there were some complex hurdles to overcome before the cameras started rolling. Amanda Thirsk, then Andrew’s private secretary and director of his Pitch@Palace, had pushed hard for Andrew to do the interview. By contrast, Jason Stein, who had been hired by Andrew in the previous September to mastermind his PR fightback, quit over the decision to go ahead with the interview against his advice.

‌



At first, says Sam, the approach to Newsnight from Andrew's camp had simply been: “An invitation to speak to him about his charity work. It's what we call in the industry a ‘puff piece’ where effectively you interview someone and they just tell you hugely flattering things about their life and then they won't speak about any of the hard news issues.”

Sam believes that Andrew’s main motivation, at that time, was his daughter Beatrice’s upcoming wedding, and wanting to dispel any whiff of scandal before walking her down the aisle. And it was Princess Beatrice’s own personal intervention, about three days before that which not only ensured that the pivotal broadcast went ahead, but also earned Andrew’s oldest daughter a significant two-word nickname from Sam.

View 3 Images Former Newsnight producer Sam McAlister (Image: PA)

‌



Beatrice had entered the negotiations, Sam says, to protect her father’s interests. And without her intervention that fateful Newsnight programme may well not have happened: “I called her The Rainmaker,” Sam said. “It was clear that if we did not answer her questions well that this interview was never going to happen.”

It might have been better for Beatrice’s father if the interview had not gone ahead. Some of Andrew’s claims went on to become national jokes, for example his reaction to the suggestion that he had been seen in a nightclub, covered in sweat, and dancing with one of Epstein’s best-known victims, Virginia Guiffre.

He told Emily Maitlis: “There's a slight problem with the sweating because I have a peculiar medical condition which is that I don't sweat …or I didn't sweat at the time because I had suffered what I would describe as an overdose of adrenalin in the Falklands War when I was shot at and … it was almost impossible for me to sweat.”

‌



View 3 Images Andrew and Emily Maitlis (Image: BBC.com)

Similarly the suggestion that Andrew, then a prince of the realm, had dined with Beatrice in the Woking branch of Pizza Express without anyone noticing either of them has also gone on to become a long-running joke at the disgraced royal’s expense.

Princess Beatrice, who is currently ninth in line for the throne, has remained close to her father, while rumours circulate that her younger sister has begun to “distance herself” from her father and his many scandals.

Article continues below

A close friend of Eugenie reportedly told US news site Page Six that the princess “feels like she’s being tarnished with a brush that’s not hers, that’s her father’s,” and that she’s “very, very frustrated.”

Another source close to the two princesses added that they “are in as good as spirits as they can be,” amid all the controversy, but added that for both of them it’s “a never-ending s***show.”

News.title: Timelapse shows change in the flow of ships in the Strait of Hormuz
News.content:
The Strait of Hormuz is a key artery for the movement of global energy supplies.

Usually, about 20% of global oil and gas passes through the narrow shipping lane in the Gulf.

Iran's General Sardar Jabbari said that Tehran will now "not let a single drop of oil leave the region".

A timelapse of marine traffic showed the flow of ships has decreased in the strait since the US and Israeli coordinated military offensive against Iran began on 28 February 2026.

Blocking the strait could further inflate the cost of goods and services worldwide, and hit some of the world's biggest economies, including China, India and Japan, which are among the top importers of crude oil passing through the waterway.

News.title: How depleted weapons stockpiles could affect the Iran conflict
News.content: Patriot missiles have been in high demand – not just from the US but also its Arab allies and Ukraine. Each interceptor missile costs more than $4m (£3m) and the US is believed to currently produce around 700 a year. If Iran is still able to launch ballistic missiles, it will eat into these limited stockpiles.

News.content: "It's not about the hours or days. We cannot move if the air above us is not cleaned. We need to see weapons depots [of Iran's security forces] being destroyed. Otherwise, it would be suicidal."

It is something which we think is reasonable for schools to pay for out of core budgets. We are committed to working with schools to make that as easy as possible."

Boxing.title: Eddie Hearn claims Conor Benn offered him money to keep quiet over his controversial decision to defect to bitter rival Dana White's Zuffa Boxing
Boxing.content:
Eddie Hearn has claimed that Conor Benn offered him money to avoid speaking negatively about their split after the British boxer signed a blockbuster deal with Zuffa Boxing.

Benn's decision to leave Hearn's Matchroom stable after a decade together came as a surprise within the industry, with the 29-year-old agreeing terms on what has been widely reported as a $15million one-fight deal with the new promotional venture headed up by Dana White.

Hearn, who guided Benn from his professional debut at 19 and oversaw his rise to world-level contention, admitted he was 'devastated' by the departure. He also revealed he was given just three days to match the offer - which he felt was 'completely unreasonable commercially'.

Despite Benn publicly insisting he would still like a relationship with his former promoter and even suggesting there could be a place for him within his team, Hearn says part of the exit discussions left him stunned and disappointed in his former star.

Speaking to iFL TV about the way in which Benn handled the situation, Hearn said: 'There was something else which was, "We'll give you a few quid if you don't say anything bad", kind of thing.

'And I was like, What? "We still want to work with you and here's a few quid".

Hearn has claimed Benn offered him money to avoid speaking negatively about their split

Benn announced he had jumped ship to Dana White's promotional company Zuffa Boxing

'And I'm just like… so I've received it and it's gone to Frank Smith and Shaun Palmer, who we're kind of the main operators within the business.

'And we're all then straight on like, "Have you seen that? Are they f***ing having a laugh?" Everyone was just completely shocked.'

Hearn also expressed frustration at how the communication was handled, revealing he first learned of developments formally through Benn's legal team rather than directly from the fighter.

He said: 'I text Conor maybe four or five days later and said, "I really think we should have a call. For everything that I've done for you, I don't think it's fair that I should have to deal with a letter from your lawyer. Let's just have a chat".

'Basically, man to man. And he said, "I heard you were quite emotional on the call. I think we should just let it all die down and talk another time".

'I'm like, "f*** me". And that was probably the moment where I just thought I cannot believe on the believe.'

While reiterating that he respects any fighter's right to maximise their earning potential, Hearn insisted his issue was not financial.

'To be honest, I think people get it a little bit mixed up between a fighter shouldn't be denied an opportunity to make a load of money for their family and all this kind of stuff,' he said.

Benn's decision to leave Hearn's Matchroom stable after a decade together came as a surprise

Conor Benn will fight Regis Prograis on April 11 on the same card as the returning Tyson Fury

'I couldn't agree more. This is not about that. This is just the way that you act to someone that has done so much for you and has basically saved your career.

'And if you listen to the Conor Benn story, I don't want to say saved his life, but the people who were there for him like that – which you can count on one hand – played a massive part in that.

'So to forget them or to treat them with disloyalty for me is unforgivable.'

Benn's next bout is scheduled for April 11 in a 150lb catchweight clash against former 140lb world champion Regis Prograis.

He is coming off two high-profile contests with Chris Eubank Jr and remains highly ranked at welterweight as he prepares to begin the next chapter of his career under new promotional leadership.

Boxing.title: Inside the incredible plans to transform the Pyramids of Giza into a heavyweight boxing venue for Oleksandr Usyk's historic fight
Boxing.content:
Oleksandr Usyk will swap packed stadiums for one of the most extraordinary settings sport has ever seen when he takes on former kickboxing champion Rico Verhoeven on May 23.

The two men are set to clash in front of the Pyramids of Giza with the WBC heavyweight world title on the line after Mauricio Sulaiman confirmed the green and gold belt will be at stake.

The WBC president has granted Usyk special permission to make a voluntary defence of his title before fulfilling his mandatory obligation, a bout against interim champion Agit Kabayel.

For Usyk, May's event will represent a slightly unconventional chapter in a career defined by taking on the biggest challenges in the most hostile environments.

Verhoeven, meanwhile, will dip his toe into boxing's ultimate proving ground and his first outing in a professional boxing ring for a decade after years of kickboxing dominance.

For organisers, it will be more than just a fight. It will be a global event, designed to merge elite combat sport with one of the most iconic backdrops on Earth.

Oleksandr Usyk will swap packed stadiums for one of the most extraordinary settings sport has ever seen when he takes on former kickboxing champion Rico Verhoeven on May 23

The two men are set to clash in front of the Pyramids of Giza (Verhoeven pictured above)

When Tiesto took to the decks on the Pyramids of Giza last December, the ancient plateau was transformed into something resembling a sci-fi movie set.

Lasers sliced through the sky, vast LED screens rose from the sand, and the pyramids themselves were illuminated like the backdrop to a Hollywood movie.

Yet for all the spectacle, what mattered most was what you couldn't see.

Behind the scenes, the entire operation ran like a military exercise. The stage was completely temporary - no digging, no drilling, no permanent fixings.

Every truss, lighting tower and screen was carefully weighted and balanced to ensure the desert surface (and the buried archaeology beneath it) remained untouched.

It's believed a fight involving Usyk would follow exactly the same blueprint: a raised, freestanding ring platform constructed entirely above ground and dismantled within hours of the final bell.

One obvious concern is noise. Heavyweight boxing is not exactly subtle.

But during Tiesto's show, engineers developed a 'heritage-safe' sound system that directed bass frequencies away from the pyramids.

When Tiesto took to the decks on the Pyramids of Giza last December, the ancient plateau was transformed into something resembling a sci-fi movie set

The WBC president has granted Usyk special permission to make a voluntary defence before fulfilling his mandatory obligation, a bout that must come against Agit Kabayel

For Usyk, May's event will represent a slightly unconventional chapter in a career defined by taking on the biggest challenges in the most hostile environments

Real-time vibration sensors monitored the limestone structures throughout the night to ensure sound levels stayed within strict archaeological limits.

A boxing fight would produce nothing like the sustained low-frequency rumble of a three-hour electronic set. Even so, vibration monitoring would almost certainly be in place again as an added safeguard.

The logistics would be just as tightly controlled. For Tiesto's event, guests entered via the Grand Egyptian Museum before being transported by dedicated shuttle to the secured concert zone on the plateau. A fight night would be expected to follow a similarly high-end access plan.

The concert footprint included a vast main arena space, VIP table sections, hospitality and brand activation zones, plus food and beverage areas spread across the sand.

For boxing, that layout could easily translate into a ringside enclosure carved into the desert, elevated hospitality decks and dramatic broadcast lighting tailored for a global pay-per-view audience.

Security, too, was uncompromising. Mandatory bag checks, controlled access points and a strict no re-entry policy were enforced throughout the concert - measures that would likely be even tighter for a world-title bout staged in the shadow of the last surviving wonder of the ancient world.

Boxing.title: Anthony Joshua takes a shot at Conor Benn in Instagram post as Brit shows his support for long-time promoter Eddie Hearn after former stablemate left Matchroom for rival Zuffa Boxing
Boxing.content:
Anthony Joshua takes a shot at Conor Benn in Instagram post as Brit shows his support for long-time promoter Eddie Hearn after former stablemate left Matchroom for rival Zuffa Boxing

Anthony Joshua has waded into the ongoing feud between Conor Benn and Eddie Hearn, lending his support to the promoter.

Benn, son of British legend Nigel, sensationally announced last month that he was ditching Hearn's promotional team Matchroom Boxing after a decade-long relationship - leaving for his rival Dana White's outfit Zuffa Boxing instead.

Hearn has since spoken out about that decision, revealing he was left 'devastated' by Benn having guided the British boxer from his 2016 debut to headlining cards and sticking by him after failing two drug tests in 2022.

And on Tuesday another long-term Matchroom Boxing fighter in Joshua showed his support to Hearn.

Uploading to his Instagram story, the former two-time heavyweight world champion shared a picture of him and Hearn dressed sharply.

Accompanied with that was a caption which read: 'AJ: Did you bring the fire power Eddie?

Anthony Joshua (left) took to Instagram on Tuesday to lend his support for Eddie Hearn (right) after Conor Benn left his Matchroom Boxing for rivals Zuffa Boxing last month

Benn (top) stunned Hearn when he decided to end their 10-year working relationship

'Eddie: It's tucked on my waste line mush [sic]

'AJ: I'll let you know when I have a clear sight of Connor Benn [sic]'.

When it was announced that he was splitting from Hearn, Benn - who has ambitions of winning the WBC welterweight title - thanked the 46-year-old for standing by him during the tough periods of his career and said he was excited for a 'new chapter' aimed at securing 'legacy fights' on the biggest stages.

However, speaking to iFL TV after Benn's bombshell announcement, Hearn claimed he was informed of the decision by the boxer's lawyer before being refused a phone call with him.

'Me personally, pretty devastated,' Hearn said of his reaction to the news. 'I have had a few days to digest what might happen. It is not often you get shocked.

'I blame myself. I forgot it was boxing. I just felt the loyalty that we have shown would never ever put us in this position.'

Daily Mail Sport reported that Hearn is thought to have lent Benn £500,000 following the collapse of his 2022 match-up with Eubank Jr, which came about due to his positive tests.

Hearn added that he 'misjudged the character' of Benn by feeling the 'loyalty' he had shown him 'would be enough to talk it through or get close to a number'.

He continued: 'It feels a little bit of a wasted few years. I gave a lot... I believed him and I backed him and I never gave in when he was done, out. Loaned him hundreds of thousands of pounds, but yeah on we go.'

'I just think the way it was done was very very wrong,' he added. 'But everyone is different. Some people, loyalty really matters, other people maybe not so.

'The way it played out to receive an email from the lawyer and then to ask for a call and not the willingness to have it, you just lose a bit of your soul to it.'



Benn's first bout as a Zuffa Boxing fighter will be against Regis Prograis on April 11 at the Tottenham Hotspur Stadium.

Boxing.title: Aussie boxer reveals that he received shocking death threats following the controversial ending to his fight with Nikita Tszyu
Boxing.content:
Aussie boxer Michael Zerafa has revealed he received multiple death threats after the controversial ending to his fight against Nikita Tszyu, adding that the online reaction ‘broke’ him.

The 33-year-old threatened to walk away from boxing following the no-contest, after he was booed out of the Brisbane Entertainment Centre in January, but has since revealed he is eager to return to challenge some of the biggest names in the sport.

Fans were left fuming after his fight against Tszyu in January ended prematurely when Zerafa had told a ringside doctor that he couldn’t see out of his left eye after accidentally clashing heads with his opponent.

Later, during a post-fight interview, Zerafa said that the ‘doctor had called it off’. The saga then took another controversial twist, with the Aussie boxer stating that his vision was just ‘blurry’, before adding that he wanted to continue fighting.

Spectators inside the Brisbane Entertainment Centre booed the Australian while some threw cans towards the ring.

Michael Zerafa (pictured) has revealed that he received 20 death threats in the 48 hours following his controversial fight with NikitaTszyu

The bout was called a no-contest after Zerafa told a ringside doctor that he couldn't see out of his left eye after clashing heads with Tszyu (left)

Tszyu has since categorically ruled out a rematch.

‘I'm closing the book on it. I'm just disappointed for everybody involved. I'm gutted for everyone,’ Tszyu said.

After the fight, Zerafa claimed in a social media post that he had suffered a shattered eye socket and damage to his retina. He had spent time in a Brisbane hospital, but his team did not disclose the doctor’s findings.

The 33-year-old has now spoken for the first time since the bout, but did not comment on the moment where he declared that he couldn’t see.

Speaking to The BillyJoe Podcast, he said: ‘Still to this day I don’t understand [the hate].

‘People say: “It’s because you b****ed it”, but I didn’t.

‘I had a genuine injury. I was winning the fight.’

After the bout, Zerafa issued an apology to fans, claiming that his choice of words was ‘poor’.

Zerafa had revealed that he had suffered a shattered eye-socket on social media following the fight

Zerafa (left) revealed that the criticism in the aftermath of the bout 'broke' him

He also doubled down on the legitimacy of his injury, stating that his vision was only 6/20. He added that ‘there was an issue with my eyesight, but it does not change the fact that my wording was poor.’

That apology has since been removed from his Instagram.

In the immediate aftermath of the bout, fans took to social media to send him abusive messages, with Zerafa revealing that some had even gone to his sponsors to ask why they were partnering with the Australian.

‘It broke me. I was on top. I’ve given my absolute all for this,’ Zerafa told the podcast. ‘When all that subsided, to see death threats, memes…

‘In that 48 hours, I reckon I copped 20 [death threats],’ he added.

‘We’re talking 48 hours. You get so much crap, man.

‘All directed at me. For what?’

He was pressed further on what kind of threats he received.

‘It was: “When I see you, I’ll make sure I’ll cut your throat, I know where you train, be expecting me there”.’

‘They reach out to sponsors and say: “As if you’re sponsoring this guy”.

‘Sometimes it makes me just want to slap these people. How much do you hate your life?

‘I get you might be jealous, but go and be jealous quietly.’

Questions have mounted over his future in the sport, with Zerafa’s relationship with No Limit Boxing said to have deteriorated.

But the 33-year-old, who holds a 34-5-0 record, added that he is rebuilding and is determined to challenge for a world title.

While he has kept a very low profile over the past two months, the fighter took to Instagram over the weekend to publish a video announcing that he was working hard to get back to competing.

He even shot a dig at those who had criticised him, writing in the caption of the video: 'And yeah... I can see now.'

Zerafa has now announced that he is hoping to get back to the ring and challenge the best in the game, poking a dig at his critics by writing on Instagram: 'And yeah... I can see now'

‘I’m not done yet. I know a lot of people would like to see me done, but I’ve still got a fire burning,’ Zerafa said during the podcast, when asked about what the future holds.

‘I’ve got a lot of people to prove wrong, I’m hungrier and better things are coming.

‘I’ve cleaned out my backyard, it’s time to get back to where I belong, back with the bigger guys, back where people know what they’re doing.

‘Boxing is a beautiful sport, but there’s too many people with their hands in it who are destroying it.

‘And I don’t even need to say names.’

Boxing.title: Revealed: Anthony Mundine's startling courtroom act as he has a bizarre clash with judge
Boxing.content:
Anthony Mundine has left a Sydney courtroom stunned after performing an acknowledgement of country and refusing to follow standard plea procedure during a tense exchange with a magistrate.

The former rugby league star and world champion boxer appeared at Sutherland Local Court last Tuesday, where he represented himself on a charge of driving during a disqualification period.

Before proceedings began, Mundine performed an acknowledgement of country and addressed the court in unusual terms.

'I'm Anthony of the tribe Mundine, here in my private capacity,' he said.

He also acknowledged the 'Dharug people of the Eora Nation'.

The court was then drawn into a procedural standoff when Mundine declined to enter a formal plea to the charge.

Anthony Mundine caused a scene when he appeared in a Sydney court over a charge of driving during a disqualification period

The former NRL and boxing star conducted an acknowledgement of country and refused to enter a plea to the charge

Magistrate Jillian Kiely explained that the legal process required a clear plea of either guilty or not guilty.

'If you fail to make a plea, I can't make a plea of not guilty,' the magistrate said.

Mundine refused to comply with the court's direction.

'I don't consent … I'm innocent,' he said.

Because Mundine would not formally enter a plea, the magistrate entered a not guilty plea on his behalf in accordance with court procedure.

The 50-year-old also attempted to have the charge dismissed, but the magistrate informed him that she did not have the authority to withdraw the matter. Only police could make that decision.

'They [police] will be held accountable … I'm joking,' he said.

The court appearance was the latest in a string of motoring offences Mundine has racked up

The court appearance follows a string of motoring offences stemming from a police stop near Mundine's home.

Authorities allege he was driving while disqualified and operating a vehicle without proper registration and insurance.

Mundine has indicated he intends to challenge the matter on broader legal grounds.

'To elaborate, the matter pertains to the attempted legal restriction of fundamental rights,' he said.

He cited 'the right to travel, the right to peace, the right to freedom of association, and the right to equality before the law, including the right to be heard and seen equally in the eyes of the law'.

After leaving the courtroom, Mundine briefly shadow-boxed for waiting media but declined to provide further explanation about his defence strategy.

Boxing.title: Inside my wild night at a Las Vegas casino with Floyd Mayweather: Boxing legend splashed out on caviar, fine whiskey and dropped $500 tips galore - but he baulked at spending his cash on post-night snack, writes Daily Mail's CHARLOTTE DALY
Boxing.content:
Former world champion Floyd Mayweather Jr has announced a surprise return to the ring, revealing plans for a fourth comeback that will begin with an exhibition against Mike Tyson before a blockbuster professional rematch with Manny Pacquiao later this year.

The rivals are set to meet again on September 19 at the Sphere - 11 years after their long-awaited 2015 showdown, when Mayweather claimed a unanimous-decision victory in the so-called 'Fight of the Century.'

Pacquiao, now 47, returned to action in July 2025 and fought Mario Barrios to a majority draw for the WBC welterweight title, insisting his comeback is driven by a desire to become world champion again. Mayweather, meanwhile, says he 'still has what it takes to set more records,' despite questions regarding his financial status.

A recent report by Business Insider suggested Mayweather’s widely publicised fortune may be overstated, citing liens, outstanding debts, foreclosures and heavily leveraged assets including property holdings and his private jet.

Back in 2024, I caught a glimpse of life inside Mayweather's orbit - a whirlwind casino evening filled with luxury excess, eye-watering tips and one surprising moment of frugality.

Floyd Mayweather invited Daily Mail Sport for a night out in Las Vegas in an extraordinary offer

Charlotte Daly hit the strip with Mayweather for a big night out in Las Vegas (pictured above)

When Floyd Mayweather invites you for a night out in Las Vegas, you know it will be extraordinary. Picture this: the neon lights of the strip reflecting off luxury cars, the pulsating energy of the city that never sleeps, and 'Money Mayweather' at the centre of it all.

From exclusive cigar lounges to high-roller tables, this was no ordinary night out. We sipped 'Good Money Whiskey' and indulged in caviar before discussing Mayweather's relationship with his father, the key moments in his career, the current crop of boxers, and his exhibition earnings.

In true Mayweather style, he arrived at Eight Lounge, a chic cigar spot, in a blacked-out SUV, wearing a £2,000 Gucci jacket and a gleaming Rolex. Fans clamoured for photos as he was greeted by models holding sparklers and a glowing Mayweather sign.

'I do like the finer things in life', Mayweather quipped when asked about his love for luxury. 'I enjoy my cars and my watches but money don't make me, I make money.

'I don't just keep all the money either, I give back to the less fortunate. My mother don't want for nothing, my loved ones don't want for anything. My team live a pretty good life. They travel the world, stay at five star hotels. But, the most important thing is, we are happy. I was happy before I made money. With or without money I am a happy person.'

A fixture on the Vegas Strip with his 7,000-square-foot gentleman's club and top-tier boxing gym, Mayweather took charge behind the bar, crafting an Old Fashioned cocktail with his Good Money Whiskey - a brand now available in 10 states including Nevada, California, and Florida.

When handed the five-year-old whiskey, Mayweather joked, 'She's come all the way from the UK, and you're giving her the five-year-old whiskey? She deserves the 10-year!'

Though not drinking himself, Mayweather passed the cocktail with a knowing smile. 'It's just like boxing,' he said confidently. 'I had the best team, so I got the best results. I've got the best team working on my brands, too. Just try it and you'll see.'

In true Mayweather style, he arrived at Eight Lounge, a chic cigar spot, in a blacked-out SUV, wearing a £2,000 Gucci jacket and a gleaming Rolex

With a drink in hand, we moved to Mayweather's exclusive area, adorned with Le Bon Argent Champagne and attentive waitresses ensuring my glass was always full. Intrigued by highlight reels of his career playing on the screens overhead, I asked the boxing legend about his favorite memory.

'I'm not sure which one was my favourite fight but the best fighter I ever fought probably was Manny Pacquiao. I can see why he won so many fights, it's all the moves he would make. But, I reckon the roughest fight I ever had was Emanuel Augustus, he was very tough competitor. When I faced him, I think he was 26 with 14 losses but extremely tough.

'That being said, I think around half of my 50 fights were world title fights, so they were all good fights'.

Mayweather faced and defeated some of the biggest names in boxing, including Manny Pacquiao, Oscar De La Hoya, and Canelo Alvarez. He also claimed world titles in five different weight classes: Super Featherweight, Lightweight, Light Welterweight, Welterweight, and Light Middleweight.

But the true highlight of his career was walking away unharmed—a poignant topic given his father and uncle, Roger, both suffered neurological damage resulting in memory loss.

'The real highlight of my career is that I am still able to communicate. That I am still able to speak well. I am still able to read and write. I am able to spend time with my children and I am able to travel. I still have all my faculties. That's the part about my career I am most pleased with. Not everyone gets that luxury and I've seen it first hand.'

Mayweather's relationship with his father has been complex but pivotal. Mayweather Sr. was instrumental in shaping his son's career, developing the defensive style that became his hallmark. Despite public disagreements and periods of estrangement, their bond remains strong.

'My dad was my inspiration growing up. Without my dad I wouldn't have any of this. Without my dad I wouldn't have the crazy real estate portfolio I have. I wouldn't have met my two best friends, James McNair and Ricki Brazil. I wouldn't be here with you today. My dad is my driving force, my dad is everything to me.'

Fans clamoured for photos with him as he made his way to Eight Lounge, a chic cigar spot

A stark contrast to when Mayweather lashed out at his father in 2011, shouting: 'You couldn't fight for s***. You weren't nothing but a motherf***ing cab driver... bum a** trainer. You weren't s*** as a fighter, so how you gonna be something as a trainer. Get the f*** out my gym f*****t!'.

While other kids played, Mayweather's childhood was a whirlwind of responsibilities, juggling time between caring for his drug-addict mother and training with his father.

'My dad was about business, ever since I was a kid. From 1985, my dad had me running five miles a day. He wouldn't let me run in sneakers either. He made me run five miles every day in combat boots while I was a child.
